utilityName: City of Goodyear Backflow Prevention and Cross-Connection Control
testingFrequency: On the city compliance cycle after installation and annually where the assembly remains in service
dueBasis: Goodyear runs a real backflow program, lets testers enter results with either a code or device serial number, and routes construction-meter, hydrant, business-owner, and fire-line questions through the city utility workflow.
coveredPropertyTypes: Other protected services in the city water system
penalties: Goodyear frames the program as a drinking-water protection obligation and routes all result-entry and shutdown questions through the city. Missing the utility process leaves the assembly outside the accepted city compliance path.
sourceExcerpt: Goodyear is a strong Arizona city because the program is operational, not generic, and it covers test entry, hydrant meters, business-owner FAQs, and fire-line coordination.
verdictSummary: Goodyear is a utility-first Arizona page because the city exposes a genuine compliance workflow instead of a thin city overview.
whoIsAffected: Goodyear services with irrigation, fire-line, hydrant-meter, construction, or other cross-connection risk that the city backflow program tracks.
residentialNotes: Residential demand is strongest where a property has an irrigation or other protected service that puts it inside the city program.
The city utility page is more useful than generic homeowner plumbing advice because it explains result-entry and enforcement mechanics.
commercialNotes: Commercial value is strong because the city openly handles business-owner FAQs, hydrant meters, and fire-line shutdown questions.
This utility broadens the Arizona cluster with a city that behaves like an actual compliance operator.

annualTesting.summary: Goodyear is useful because testers can file results through the city process even when the customer only has the device serial number.
highlights: The city explicitly supports backflow result entry.
Codes are optional if the device serial number is known.
The utility page is built around real city-side workflow questions.
workflowSteps: Confirm the assembly is in the Goodyear program.
Enter the results through the city workflow using the code or serial number.

irrigation.summary: Goodyear is strong for irrigation because the utility bundles irrigation, business-owner, and meter questions on the same backflow surface.
highlights: Irrigation is one of the clearest local triggers.
The city page supports real city-side testing questions.
This utility should convert better than generic irrigation content.

fireLine.summary: Goodyear also supports fire-line and shutdown intent because the utility explicitly addresses fire-line and shutdown questions on the same program page.
highlights: The city calls out shutdown questions for work on assemblies.
Hydrant and construction-meter flows are already part of the same utility surface.
This is a stronger city page than a bare code citation.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the work touches a fire line or shutdown.
Coordinate with the city utility workflow.
Complete the required testing and result entry.
Check whether the assembly is inside Goodyear's program.
Use the city's result-entry workflow with the code or device serial number.
Coordinate shutdown or hydrant-meter questions with the utility before the work starts.
failureHighlights: Goodyear exposes a real result-entry workflow.
The city handles fire-line, hydrant-meter, and business-owner questions on the same surface.
This is a stronger utility page than generic city FAQ content.

costBand.testingRange: Testing is market-priced, but the local value is a clear city-side filing path.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest cost depends on assembly type and whether shutdown or fire-side coordination is required.
costBand.pricingNotes: The strongest signal is that the utility already behaves like an operator, not an educational brochure.
